title: The Peasant Emperor
bullets: Liu Bang, a minor village constable with no military training, became a rebel after accidentally letting convicts escape under Qin law, which made the offense itself punishable by death.
The four-year Chu-Han Contention pitted Liu Bang against the aristocratic general Xiang Yu, who won most battles but alienated every ally through impulsiveness and paranoid cruelty.
At Gaixia in 202 BCE, Han soldiers sang Chu folk songs around Xiang Yu's camp as psychological warfare, triggering mass desertions that ended the civil war in a single night.
Liu Bang kept every Qin institution — the county system, standardized script, centralized bureaucracy — but replaced Legalist terror with Huang-Lao Daoism's hands-off governing philosophy.
Chang'an, rebuilt near the ruins of the Qin capital, grew into one of the ancient world's largest cities, with the Weiyang Palace covering more area than the Forbidden City and Versailles combined.
The name Han, originally a contemptuous assignment from Xiang Yu, became the identity of an ethnicity — ninety percent of China's 1.4 billion people today identify as Han Chinese.
Liu Bang enfeoffed relatives and allies as kings over large territories, then spent his remaining years purging the non-Liu kings when their power grew threatening.
Gaozu died in 195 BCE at sixty-two, having built a dynasty that would last 407 years and survive one complete interruption under Wang Mang's Xin interregnum.

title: Empress and Heirs
bullets: Empress Lu, China's first female power holder, mutilated her husband's favorite concubine as a 'human swine' and ruled through puppet emperors for fifteen years until her death in 180 BCE.
Emperor Wen cut taxes to one-thirtieth of the harvest and governed through Huang-Lao 'non-action,' producing such prosperity that grain piled up until the coin strings rotted from disuse.
Chunyu Tiying's petition to sell herself into slavery to spare her father moved Emperor Wen to abolish the most brutal corporal punishments, including foot amputation and branding.
The young intellectual Jia Yi warned that the feudal kingdoms would revolt and that the Xiongnu heqin policy was unsustainable — he was right about everything but died at thirty-three in exile.
The Rebellion of the Seven Kingdoms in 154 BCE lasted just eighty-seven days after Zhou Yafu cut the rebels' grain supply instead of fighting them directly.
After Baideng in 200 BCE, where Modu Chanyu trapped Emperor Gaozu for seven days, the Han sent annual tribute of silk, grain, and princesses to the Xiongnu for sixty years under the heqin system.
Modu Chanyu sent Empress Lu a deliberately obscene marriage proposal after Gaozu's death, and she swallowed the insult because the Han military could not match Xiongnu cavalry.
By 141 BCE Emperor Jing had stripped the feudal kingdoms of administrative authority, leaving his successor Emperor Wu a genuinely centralized empire ready for aggressive expansion.

title: The Martial Emperor
bullets: Emperor Wu ruled for fifty-four years, reversed sixty years of Xiongnu appeasement, established Confucianism as state ideology, opened the Silk Road, and nearly bankrupted the empire doing it all.
Dong Zhongshu's synthesis gave the emperor cosmic authority while letting Confucian scholars interpret natural disasters as divine criticism, creating a permanent check on imperial power.
The Imperial Academy, founded in 124 BCE with fifty students, grew to thirty thousand by the Eastern Han — the world's first state university for training a civil bureaucracy.
Zhang Qian spent ten years as a Xiongnu prisoner before escaping to complete his diplomatic mission, which failed militarily but accidentally opened the Silk Road to Central Asia.
Huo Qubing seized the Hexi Corridor at barely twenty years old, cutting the Xiongnu off from their western allies and opening China's permanent road to Central Asia before dying at twenty-four.
The Battle of Mobei in 119 BCE drove the Xiongnu beyond the Gobi permanently, with Han generals performing victory sacrifices to Chinese gods on Xiongnu sacred mountains.
Emperor Wu nationalized salt and iron production to fund his wars, and the resulting monopoly outlasted the Han by two millennia, persisting through every subsequent dynasty until 1949.
Su Wu refused to renounce his Han allegiance during nineteen years of captivity near Lake Baikal, clinging to his diplomatic staff until a clever lie about a goose secured his release.
The Repentance Edict of Luntai in 89 BCE was one of the only times a Chinese emperor formally admitted policy failure, suspending further military campaigns and ordering agricultural recovery.

title: The Grand Historian
bullets: Sima Qian defended General Li Ling's surrender to the Xiongnu and was sentenced to castration — the most degrading non-capital punishment — by Emperor Wu, who took the defense as personal criticism.

Sima Qian chose to live through his punishment because his unfinished history mattered more than his dignity, explaining his decision in a private letter that is one of the ancient world's most moving documents.
The Shiji covered 2,500 years of Chinese history in 130 chapters and invented the biographical approach to history, telling the past through individual lives rather than administrative chronology.

Sima Qian's portrait of Xiang Yu is one of world literature's great tragic character studies, while his portrait of Liu Bang captures the lucky peasant whose greatest talent was knowing what he didn't know.
The Old Text versus New Text controversy consumed Han intellectual life for centuries, with competing versions of the classics carrying different political implications about imperial authority.
The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art solved simultaneous equations using a method equivalent to modern Gaussian elimination, two thousand years before Gauss developed his version.

The Discourses on Salt and Iron, convened in 81 BCE, recorded Confucian scholars and government officials debating whether the state should engage in commerce at all — one of the ancient world's most remarkable economic documents.
The south-pointing spoon demonstrated understanding of magnetic polarity for feng shui purposes, though the step from ritual compass to navigational tool would take another thousand years.

title: Wang Mang and the Interregnum
bullets: After Emperor Wu's death, Huo Guang served as regent for three consecutive emperors, deposing one after just twenty-seven days and charging him with 1,127 offenses.
Emperor Xuan, raised as a commoner after his family was destroyed in a palace purge, established the Western Regions Protectorate in 60 BCE, formalizing Han control over the Silk Road city-states.
Wang Zhaojun traveled north to marry the Xiongnu chanyu after Emperor Yuan saw her beauty too late at the departure ceremony — her story became Chinese literature's archetype of border sacrifice.

Wang Mang spent decades building an impeccable Confucian reputation before seizing the throne in 9 CE, then attempted history's most ambitious reform program based entirely on readings of classical texts.
Wang Mang reformed the currency five times in fifteen years, introducing twenty-eight denominations that destroyed commercial trust and essentially annihilated the merchant class.
The Yellow River's catastrophic course change in 11 CE flooded millions of hectares and triggered the famines that produced the Red Eyebrow and Lulin rebellions, dooming Wang Mang's government.

Liu Xiu, a seventh-generation descendant of Emperor Gaozu who had grown up as a commoner farmer, spent twelve years defeating rival warlords to restore the Han dynasty from the new capital at Luoyang.

Emperor Guangwu demobilized most of his army after reunification, reportedly saying he wanted his generals to die of old age in their beds — the most successful post-war civil-military transition in Chinese history.

title: The Eastern Han
bullets: Emperor Ming dreamed of a golden man radiating light, leading to envoys returning from India with monks, scriptures, and the founding of the White Horse Temple around 67 CE — China's first Buddhist monastery.

Buddhist translators rendered Sanskrit concepts using Daoist vocabulary, making nirvana 'non-action' and dharma 'the Way,' which made Buddhism comprehensible but subtly changed what Chinese audiences received.
The Trung Sisters led a revolt against Han rule in Vietnam in 40 CE, briefly liberating sixty-five cities before General Ma Yuan's twenty thousand soldiers suppressed them in 43 CE.
Ban Chao spent thirty-one years in Central Asia with never more than a few thousand men, reconquering the Western Regions through audacity, diplomacy, and precisely targeted violence.
Ban Chao sent Gan Ying toward Rome in 97 CE, but Parthian merchants at the Persian Gulf discouraged him by claiming the voyage took two years and could kill him with homesickness.
Ban Gu compiled the Book of Han, the first single-dynasty history and model for twenty-three subsequent official dynastic histories, but died in prison when his patron Dou Xian fell from power.
Ban Zhao completed her brother's unfinished history and authored Lessons for Women, the most influential text on female conduct in Chinese history — while herself holding an official court position.

Cai Lun standardized paper manufacture using tree bark, hemp, rags, and fishing nets in 105 CE, creating the cheap writing material whose spread from China to the Islamic world to Europe enabled mass literacy.
Dou Xian crushed the Northern Xiongnu at Ikh Bayan in 89 CE and inscribed a victory stele on Wolf Xu Mountain, but was forced to commit suicide three years later in a palace coup by Emperor He.

title: Buddhism, Daoism, and the Mandate Fraying
bullets: Between Emperor He's death in 106 CE and the end of the dynasty, the Eastern Han had ten emperors — seven came to the throne as children, trapping the court in cycles of regent families versus eunuch factions.

Around 148 CE the Parthian prince An Shigao arrived in Luoyang and began the first systematic translation of Buddhist scriptures into Chinese, focusing on meditation practices with no Confucian equivalent.

Zhang Daoling founded the Celestial Masters school of religious Daoism in 142 CE with formal parishes, clergy, and sacraments — the first Chinese religion with an institutional structure rather than temple-by-temple practice.

Wang Chong argued systematically that natural events have natural causes, that ghosts do not exist, and that the emperor's behavior does not cause floods — extraordinarily rational positions for any ancient culture.

Zhang Heng's seismograph detected a distant earthquake in 132 CE that no one in Luoyang had felt, and the court mocked him until messengers confirmed a major quake in the exact direction his instrument had indicated.

Zheng Xuan synthesized the competing Old Text and New Text schools of Confucian interpretation into a unified commentary tradition so authoritative that the Tang dynasty made his versions the official examination texts.
The Partisan Prohibitions of 166 CE banned the empire's best-educated officials from government after eunuchs convinced Emperor Huan that the Confucian scholars were forming a conspiracy.

The Taiping Jing scripture promised a chosen leader would establish a new age of universal peace, and in the famine-struck eastern provinces, millions of listeners organized into thirty-six regional cells awaiting the signal.

title: The Yellow Turbans and the End
bullets: In spring 184 CE, coordinated uprisings erupted simultaneously across eight provinces under the slogan 'The azure heaven is dead; the yellow heaven shall rise,' with rebels wearing yellow headbands for identification.

The Han court suppressed the Yellow Turban Rebellion within a year by authorizing local magnates to raise private armies — the decision that guaranteed the dynasty's destruction, since those armies were never disbanded.

Dong Zhuo marched into Luoyang with his army in 189 CE, deposed the child emperor, installed a puppet, and burned the capital — its great libraries, palaces, and the thirty-thousand-student Imperial Academy — when rivals mobilized against him.
Cao Cao gained control of Emperor Xian in 196 CE and used him as a political tool for twenty-five years, issuing every order and eliminating every rival under the fiction of Han imperial command.

The physician Hua Tuo reportedly invented general anesthesia using mafeisan, performed abdominal surgeries, and was executed by Cao Cao for refusing to become his personal doctor — his medical texts were lost forever.
The Battle of Red Cliffs in 208 CE destroyed Cao Cao's fleet and stopped his reunification project permanently, making the three-way division between Wei, Shu Han, and Wu inevitable.
Cao Pi orchestrated Emperor Xian's formal abdication in 220 CE through an elaborate ceremony designed to look like a legitimate transfer of the Mandate of Heaven rather than a usurpation.

The Han left every institution subsequent dynasties used — Confucian examination, county administration, dynastic history format — and gave the Chinese people their name: Han Chinese, Han script, Han language.
